relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Before the story begins, the Word already is, turned toward the Divine and sharing divine being.
The Word is with the Divine from the beginning.
All becoming flows through the Word.
Life in the Word becomes light for humanity.
Light shines into darkness, and darkness cannot master it.
John appears as one sent from the Divine.
His purpose is witness so trust may awaken through the light.
John is not the light; he points toward it.
The true light illuminates every human entering the world.
The world made through him fails to know him.
He comes home, and home does not receive him.
Those who receive become children of the Divine through trust in the name.
This birth is from the Divine rather than ordinary human origin.
The Word enters embodied life and dwells among us like sacred tent-presence.
John witnesses that the coming one is before him.
Fullness pours out as grace upon grace.
Moses gives Law; Jesus Messiah brings grace and truth into arrival.
The unseen Divine is made known from the intimacy of the Father.
John’s witness begins under questioning from Jerusalem.
He refuses false identity: I am not the Messiah.
He also refuses Elijah and the Prophet labels.
They press him to name himself.
He names himself voice in wilderness, making the Lord’s way straight.
The questioners come from Pharisee circles.
They ask why he baptizes without the expected titles.
John’s water work points to the unknown one already among them.
He is not worthy even to loose the sandal of the coming one.
This witness unfolds beyond the Jordan.
John sees Jesus and names him Lamb of the Divine, bearing away the world’s misalignment.
The later-appearing one is first in depth.
John’s water baptism reveals him to Israel.
The Spirit descends like a dove and remains.
The one on whom Spirit remains baptizes in holy Spirit.
John has seen and witnesses: Son of the Divine.
John stands again with two disciples.
His gaze names Jesus again as Lamb of the Divine.
Hearing witness, the disciples follow Jesus.
Jesus asks the first Johannine question: What are you seeking?
Come and see opens the path of remaining with him.
Andrew is one of the first who heard and followed.
Andrew finds Simon with discovery: we have found Messiah.
Jesus sees Simon and names him Cephas, Rock.
Philip receives the direct call: follow me.
Philip’s origin connects him with Andrew and Peter.
Philip finds Nathanael with Moses-and-prophets recognition.
Nazareth skepticism is answered with come and see.
Jesus sees Nathanael as an Israelite without deceit.
The fig tree becomes the place of hidden seeing.
Nathanael confesses Son of the Divine and King of Israel.
Trust from being seen opens to greater seeing.
Heaven opens over the Son of Humanity, with messengers ascending and descending.
